arXiv_2104.01955v1.title: Automating Transfer Credit Assessment in Student Mobility - A Natural Language Processing-based Approach
arXiv_2104.01955v1.research_idea: This paper proposes a model to automate the assessment of transfer credits for student mobility using Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, specifically leveraging semantic and taxonomic similarity of learning outcomes (LOs).
arXiv_2104.01955v1.research_question: How can NLP techniques be used to automate the assessment of transfer credits between courses for student mobility?
hypotheses: Using semantic similarity measures based on transformer models can effectively assess the similarity between learning outcomes.
Combining taxonomic similarity (based on Bloom's taxonomy) with semantic similarity can improve the accuracy of course-to-course similarity assessment.
arXiv_2104.01955v1.data_source: A new benchmark dataset containing seven course-to-course similarity measures was proposed due to the lack of quality benchmark datasets.
arXiv_2104.01955v1.data_description: The dataset contains learning outcomes (LOs) from courses, categorized based on Bloom's taxonomy, and includes semantic and taxonomic similarity measures between courses.

independent_variables.name: semantic_similarity
independent_variables.definition: Similarity between learning outcomes based on transformer-based language models like RoBERTa

independent_variables.name: taxonomic_similarity
independent_variables.definition: Similarity between learning outcomes based on Bloom's taxonomy levels

dependent_variables.name: course_similarity
dependent_variables.formula: Aggregation of semantic and taxonomic similarity scores between courses

control_variables.name: domain_expertise
control_variables.rationale: To account for potential bias in manual assessments by domain experts

control_variables.name: institution_type
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in course structure and learning outcomes across institutions

statistical_method.specification: The model uses a clustering-inspired methodology to aggregate semantic and taxonomic similarity scores.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the effectiveness of the model in identifying similar and dissimilar courses based on learning outcomes.

result_claims.value: Higher similarity scores for courses with aligned learning outcomes
Tunable parameters allow for scenario-specific adjustments

partial_results.note: The dataset is newly proposed and may require further validation for broader applicability.

conclusion_claims: The proposed model effectively automates the assessment of transfer credits by leveraging semantic and taxonomic similarity of learning outcomes.
The model provides tunable parameters to accommodate different decision-making scenarios, offering flexibility to administrators.
limitations: The lack of quality benchmark datasets may limit the generalizability of the model.
The model's performance may be influenced by the inherent complexity and domain specificity of learning outcomes.

robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the model's flexibility and performance under different scenarios
To validate the model's accuracy against human judgments

_source.title: Automating Transfer Credit Assessment in Student Mobility -- A Natural Language Processing-based Approach

fletcher2024_reproducibility.title: Reproducibility study of “LICO: Explainable Models with Language-Image Consistency”
fletcher2024_reproducibility.research_idea: This paper investigates the reproducibility of the LICO method, which aims to improve model interpretability and classification performance by leveraging natural language supervision from a vision-language model.
fletcher2024_reproducibility.research_question: Can the claims made by Lei et al. (2023) regarding the LICO method be reproduced, particularly in terms of improved classification performance and interpretability?
hypotheses: Training an image classifier with LICO consistently improves quantitative and qualitative model interpretability.
Training an image classifier with LICO consistently improves classification accuracy.
Tasks with more classification categories benefit more from LICO because the prompt guidance comes from a manifold consisting of more classes.
fletcher2024_reproducibility.data_source: CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and subsets of ImageNet
fletcher2024_reproducibility.data_description: CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 are benchmark datasets for image classification with 10 and 100 classes, respectively. ImageNet is a large-scale image dataset with subsets used for evaluation.

independent_variables.name: LICO_training
independent_variables.definition: Whether the model was trained with the LICO method (1) or without it (0).

independent_variables.name: number_of_classes
independent_variables.definition: The number of classification categories in the dataset (e.g., 10 for CIFAR-10, 100 for CIFAR-100).

dependent_variables.name: classification_accuracy
dependent_variables.formula: Proportion of correctly classified images.

dependent_variables.name: interpretability_score
dependent_variables.formula: Quantitative measure of interpretability derived from Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, and RISE methods.

control_variables.name: model_architecture
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in performance are not due to variations in model architecture.

control_variables.name: training_procedure
control_variables.rationale: To ensure that differences in performance are not due to variations in training procedures.

statistical_method.specification: Comparative analysis of classification accuracy and interpretability scores between models trained with and without LICO.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the performance and interpretability of models trained with and without the LICO method.

partial_results.note: The effect of LICO on tasks with more classification categories could not be conclusively determined due to incomplete data.

conclusion_claims: The claims made by Lei et al. (2023) regarding the LICO method could not be consistently reproduced.
The study highlights the importance of rigorous evaluation and transparent reporting in interpretability research.
limitations: The original codebase was incomplete, requiring manual implementation of certain components.
The study focused on a subset of the original experiments, which may not fully capture the scope of the LICO method.

robustness_checks.method: Comparison with multiple interpretability methods (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, RISE)
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not dependent on a single interpretability method.

robustness_checks.method: Evaluation on multiple datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet subsets)
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are generalizable across different image classification tasks.

_source.title: Reproducibility study of "LICO: Explainable Models with Language-Image Consistency"

arts2021_natural_language_processing.title: Natural language processing to identify the creation and impact of new technologies in patent text: Code, data, and new measures
arts2021_natural_language_processing.research_idea: The paper develops natural language processing (NLP) techniques to identify the creation and impact of new technologies in U.S. patents, and validates these techniques against traditional metrics based on patent classification and citations.
arts2021_natural_language_processing.research_question: Can natural language processing techniques improve the identification of the creation and impact of new technologies in patents compared to traditional metrics?
hypotheses: NLP-based measures will better identify patents with high novelty and impact compared to traditional metrics.
NLP-based measures will show weak correlation with traditional metrics based on patent classification and citations.
arts2021_natural_language_processing.data_source: USPTO, patent claims research dataset, PATSTAT
arts2021_natural_language_processing.data_description: The dataset includes all granted U.S. utility patents from March 1969 to May 2018 (n=6,252,916). It includes titles, abstracts, and claims of patents.

independent_variables.name: new_word
independent_variables.definition: Number of unique keywords (unigrams) in a patent that appear for the first time in the U.S. patent database based on filing date.

independent_variables.name: new_bigram
independent_variables.definition: Number of unique bigrams in a patent that appear for the first time in the U.S. patent database based on filing date.

independent_variables.name: new_trigram
independent_variables.definition: Number of unique trigrams in a patent that appear for the first time in the U.S. patent database based on filing date.

independent_variables.name: new_keyword_combination
independent_variables.definition: Number of unique keyword combinations in a patent that appear for the first time in the U.S. patent database based on filing date.

independent_variables.name: cosine_similarity
independent_variables.definition: Cosine similarity between the technical content of a focal patent and all prior patents.

dependent_variables.name: new_word_reuse
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patents reusing the new keywords from a focal patent.

dependent_variables.name: new_bigram_reuse
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patents reusing the new bigrams from a focal patent.

dependent_variables.name: new_trigram_reuse
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patents reusing the new trigrams from a focal patent.

dependent_variables.name: new_keyword_combination_reuse
dependent_variables.formula: Number of patents reusing the new keyword combinations from a focal patent.

dependent_variables.name: cosine_similarity_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Cosine similarity between the content of a focal patent and all succeeding patents.

control_variables.name: patent_class
control_variables.rationale: Patent classification is used as a control variable to account for the subject matter of the patent.

control_variables.name: citation_count
control_variables.rationale: Citation count is used as a control variable to account for the prior art referenced by the patent.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses descriptive statistics and case-control study designs to validate the NLP-based measures against traditional metrics.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable

grouping_strategy.rationale: Awards-linked patents are expected to cover radically new technologies with a major impact, while rejected patents are expected to lack novelty and have little impact.

partial_results.note: The weak correlation suggests that NLP-based measures capture different aspects of novelty and impact compared to traditional metrics.

conclusion_claims: NLP-based measures outperform traditional metrics based on patent classification and citations in identifying the creation and impact of new technologies in patents.
New keyword combinations and their reuse by later patents show the strongest discriminatory power to classify both patents linked to prestigious awards and rejected patents.
limitations: The paper does not provide a detailed analysis of the limitations of the NLP-based measures.
The validation is based on case-control studies and may not generalize to all patents.

robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the NLP-based measures against traditional metrics using two distinct groups of patents: awards-linked patents and rejected patents.

_source.title: Natural Language Processing to identify the creation and impact of new technologies in patent text

arXiv_2606.04716v1.title: The State of Peer Review in Empirical Software Engineering: A Community Survey on Review Load, Quality, and GenAI Use
arXiv_2606.04716v1.research_idea: The paper investigates the current state of peer review in empirical software engineering (ESE) research, focusing on review load, quality, challenges, and the use of generative AI (GenAI) in the reviewing process. It aims to gather community insights to inform evidence-based improvements to the peer review system.
arXiv_2606.04716v1.research_question: What is the current state of peer review in the ESE community, including perceived review load, review quality, challenges, and the use of LLM-based tools, and what suggestions do community members have for improvement?
arXiv_2606.04716v1.data_source: Questionnaire survey responses collected from ESE researchers and reviewers.
arXiv_2606.04716v1.data_description: 120 responses from participants who had acted as peer reviewers in the past 12 months and were active in ESE research. Data included demographic information, perceived review load, review quality assessments, LLM usage, and suggestions for improvement.

filters: Participants must have reviewed at least one scientific paper in the past 12 months and be active in ESE research.

independent_variables.name: review_load
independent_variables.definition: Perceived review load rated on a 5-point ordinal scale from 'very low' to 'very high'.

independent_variables.name: review_type
independent_variables.definition: Type of review (journal, conference, workshop).

independent_variables.name: role
independent_variables.definition: Participant's role (tenured academic, postdoc, non-tenured academic, industry, PhD student).

independent_variables.name: llm_usage
independent_variables.definition: Whether participants use LLM-based tools in the reviewing process.

dependent_variables.name: review_quality
dependent_variables.definition: Perceived quality of reviews, including challenges and issues with the review process.

dependent_variables.name: number_of_reviews
dependent_variables.definition: Number of papers reviewed in the last year across workshops, conferences, and journals.

control_variables.name: experience
control_variables.rationale: Years of experience in ESE research to account for differences in review load and quality perception.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive statistics and thematic analysis of free-text responses to summarize the data.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare differences in review load, quality perception, and LLM usage across different career stages and roles.

key_quantities: average number of reviews per year

result_claims.value: High or very high for 80% of participants
Average of 25-32 reviews per person per year
Not explicitly quantified but discussed as a concern

partial_results.note: The extent and impact of LLM use in ESE review processes were not systematically quantified in the survey.

conclusion_claims: The ESE community is experiencing high review loads, with most participants rating their review load as high or very high.
Review quality is a concern, with challenges such as superficial evaluations and inconsistency.
The use of LLMs in the reviewing process is a growing concern, with potential impacts on review integrity and depth.
Community suggestions for improvement include adopting double-anonymous or open reviewing models, structured review forms, and reviewer training programs.
limitations: The sample is skewed towards senior researchers from Europe and North America, which may limit the generalizability of the findings.
The survey did not systematically quantify the extent and impact of LLM use in the ESE review process.
The study is based on self-reported data, which may be subject to social desirability bias.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure transparency and replicability of qualitative findings.

_source.title: The State of Peer Review in Empirical Software Engineering: A Community Survey on Review Load, Quality, and GenAI Use

shirouyeh2023_predicting_star_scientists.title: Predicting Star Scientists in the Field of Artificial Intelligence: A Machine Learning Approach
shirouyeh2023_predicting_star_scientists.research_idea: This study proposes a machine learning model to predict star scientists in the field of artificial intelligence by analyzing early-career features, network measures, and diversity metrics. The model highlights the importance of collaboration, diversity, and research output in identifying rising stars.
shirouyeh2023_predicting_star_scientists.research_question: What features can be used to predict star scientists in the field of artificial intelligence, and how do these features differentiate rising stars from their non-rising counterparts?
hypotheses: Rising stars in AI will exhibit distinct patterns in early-career features compared to non-rising stars.
Features such as gender and ethnic diversity will significantly impact an author's career development and success.
The number of articles, group discipline diversity, and weighted degree centrality will be the most important predictors of star scientists in AI.

independent_variables.name: number_of_articles
independent_variables.definition: The total number of articles published by the researcher.

independent_variables.name: group_discipline_diversity
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the diversity of disciplines within the research group of the scientist.

independent_variables.name: weighted_degree_centrality
independent_variables.definition: A network measure indicating the importance of a researcher in their scientific network, weighted by the strength of connections.

independent_variables.name: gender
independent_variables.definition: The gender of the researcher.

independent_variables.name: ethnic_diversity
independent_variables.definition: The ethnic background of the researcher.

dependent_variables.name: star_scientist_status
dependent_variables.formula: 1 if the researcher is identified as a star scientist, 0 otherwise.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in research output and recognition.

control_variables.name: institution_rank
control_variables.rationale: To control for the influence of the researcher's affiliated institution on their success.

statistical_method.specification: A machine learning model is used to predict star scientists, incorporating features such as network measures, diversity metrics, and research output.
statistical_method.estimation: Machine learning algorithms (not specified in detail) are used for prediction.

network_construction.construction_method: The network is built based on coauthorship relationships among researchers in the field of AI, with edges weighted by the frequency and strength of collaborations.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the early-career features of researchers who become star scientists with those who do not, in order to identify distinguishing characteristics.

partial_results.note: The specific data sources used in the study are not detailed in the provided text.
The exact machine learning algorithm used for prediction is not specified in the provided text.

conclusion_claims: Rising stars in AI follow different patterns in early-career features compared to non-rising stars.
The most important features in predicting star scientists in AI are the number of articles, group discipline diversity, and weighted degree centrality.
limitations: The study does not specify the exact data sources used, which limits the reproducibility of the findings.
The specific machine learning algorithm used for prediction is not detailed, making it difficult to assess the model's performance and robustness.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the model's predictions are reliable and not overfit to the training data.
To assess the impact of varying input features on the model's predictions.

gross2019_contest_models.title: Contest models highlight inherent inefficiencies of scientific funding competitions
gross2019_contest_models.research_idea: The paper uses contest theory to analyze the efficiency of scientific funding competitions and compares them with partially randomized alternatives like lotteries. It finds that the effort wasted on proposal writing can be comparable to the scientific value of funded research, especially when few proposals are funded.
gross2019_contest_models.research_question: To what extent does the community’s investment in proposal preparation negate the scientific impact of the funding program? Are there alternative mechanisms for awarding funds that advance science more efficiently?
hypotheses: The effort researchers waste in writing proposals may be comparable to the total scientific value of the research that the funding supports.

When professional pressures motivate investigators to seek funding for reasons beyond the value of the proposed science, the entire program can hamper scientific progress when the number of awards is small.

independent_variables.name: proposal_strength
independent_variables.definition: The strength of a proposal, denoted x ≥ 0, which corresponds to how much effort is invested in writing the proposal.

independent_variables.name: scientific_value
independent_variables.definition: The intrinsic scientific value of a project idea, denoted v ≥ 0, which combines the ability of the investigator and the promise of the idea itself.

independent_variables.name: extra_scientific_reward
independent_variables.definition: An additional reward for investigators, denoted v0 ≥ 0, such as prestige or promotion, which is private and not shared with the funder.

independent_variables.name: number_of_awards
independent_variables.definition: The number of proposals that can be funded, which affects the competition and the effort required to win.

dependent_variables.name: net_scientific_progress
dependent_variables.formula: The total scientific value of funded projects minus the scientific waste from proposal writing.

dependent_variables.name: proposal_effort
dependent_variables.formula: The effort (disutility cost) invested in writing proposals, denoted c(v, x) = g(v)h(x).

control_variables.name: intrinsic_scientific_value_of_proposal_writing
control_variables.rationale: A proportion k ∈ [0, 1) of the disutility cost c(v, x) is recouped by honing ideas, which is considered as the intrinsic scientific value of writing a proposal.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a contest model based on economic theory to analyze the efficiency of funding competitions and compare them with lotteries.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the efficiency of traditional proposal competitions with partially randomized alternatives like lotteries.

result_claims.value: The effort wasted in writing proposals may be comparable to the total scientific value of the research that the funding supports.

When professional pressures motivate investigators to seek funding for reasons beyond the value of the proposed science, the entire program can hamper scientific progress when the number of awards is small.

partial_results.note: The results depend on the number of proposals funded and the presence of extra-scientific incentives.

conclusion_claims: The effort researchers waste in writing proposals may be comparable to the total scientific value of the research that the funding supports, especially when only a few proposals can be funded.
Lost efficiency may be restored either by partial lotteries for funding or by funding researchers based on past scientific success instead of proposals for future work.
limitations: The model assumes a large number of scientists and predictable competition strength, which may not always hold in practice.
The model does not account for variability in reviewer opinions or other real-world complexities in the funding process.

robustness_checks.method: Comparing the model with alternative funding mechanisms like lotteries
robustness_checks.rationale: To assess the robustness of the findings to different assumptions about the funding process.

fitzgerald2021_localization.title: Is academia becoming more localised? The growth of regional knowledge networks within international research collaboration
fitzgerald2021_localization.research_idea: The paper investigates whether international research collaboration is becoming more localized over time, with a focus on the formation of regional knowledge networks and their alignment with historical geopolitical or colonial ties.
fitzgerald2021_localization.research_question: Is there an increasing trend in regional knowledge networks within international research collaboration, and how do these networks align with historical or geographical factors?
hypotheses: International research collaboration is becoming more localized over time.
Regional knowledge networks are increasingly aligned with geographical regions rather than historical geopolitical or colonial lines.
fitzgerald2021_localization.data_description: A dataset of international collaboratively authored scientific publications covering a range of disciplines published between 1970 and 2018.

independent_variables.name: time_period
independent_variables.definition: The year of publication, used to analyze trends over time.

dependent_variables.name: collaboration_intensity
dependent_variables.formula: The number of co-authorship collaborations between countries within a region versus between regions.

control_variables.name: historical_geopolitical_alliances
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of historical ties on collaboration patterns.

statistical_method.specification: Application of network science methods including historical profile clustering, entropy of collaborations, community detection, and mutual information comparisons.

network_construction.construction_method: Based on co-authorship data from Scopus, with edges representing collaborative publications between countries.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the alignment of collaboration networks with historical geopolitical lines versus geographical regions.

partial_results.note: The paper does not provide detailed disciplinary breakdowns of the results.

conclusion_claims: International research collaboration has become more localized over the past five decades, with an increase in within-region collaboration relative to international collaboration.
The alignment of collaboration networks with geographical regions has increased since 2000, while historical geopolitical or colonial lines have become less relevant.
limitations: The study is based on Scopus data, which may not capture all scientific publications, especially from non-English or non-Western sources.
The analysis does not provide a detailed breakdown of collaboration patterns by specific disciplines.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the stability of detected communities over time.
To quantify the diversity and localization of collaboration patterns.

ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.title: Retracted papers by Iranian authors: Causes, journals, time lags, affiliations, collaborations
ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.research_idea: This study investigates the causes, journals, time lags, affiliations, and collaborations of retracted papers by Iranian authors indexed in Scopus from 2001 to 2019. The findings highlight the prevalence of fake peer review and plagiarism as major causes of retractions.
ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.research_question: What are the causes, journals, time lags, affiliations, and collaborations associated with retracted papers by Iranian authors?
hypotheses: The majority of retracted papers by Iranian authors are due to fake peer review and plagiarism.
Islamic Azad University is the leading institution in terms of retracted papers.
International collaborations involving Iranian authors are associated with a significant number of retractions.
ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.data_source: Scopus database and Retraction Watch database
ghorbi2023_iranian_retractions.data_description: The study analyzed 343 retraction notices indexed in Scopus from 2001 to 2019, related to scientific articles (co-)written by at least one author affiliated with an Iranian institution. The data were merged with the Retraction Watch database to determine the reasons for retractions.

filters: articles with at least one Iranian author affiliation

independent_variables.name: retraction_reason
independent_variables.definition: The reason for the retraction, such as fake peer review, plagiarism, duplicate publication, etc.

independent_variables.name: journal
independent_variables.definition: The journal in which the retracted paper was published.

independent_variables.name: institution
independent_variables.definition: The institution affiliated with the author(s) of the retracted paper.

independent_variables.name: collaboration
independent_variables.definition: Whether the paper was the result of an international collaboration.

dependent_variables.name: retraction_count
dependent_variables.formula: The number of retracted papers associated with a given reason, journal, institution, or collaboration.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in retraction rates.

statistical_method.specification: The data were analyzed using Excel 2016 and IBM-SPSS version 24.0. Descriptive statistics were used to summarize the data, and VOSviewer software was used for visualization.

groups: papers retracted due to fake peer review
papers retracted due to duplicate publication
grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the time lags between publication and retraction for different retraction reasons.

partial_results.note: The study does not specify who initiated the retraction for 53% of the retracted papers.

conclusion_claims: The peer review system appears to be a weak point in the submission/publication process, as most retractions were due to fake peer review and plagiarism.
Improving the peer review system and increasing editorial control could likely reduce the number of retractions.
limitations: The study is limited to retracted papers indexed in Scopus and Retraction Watch, which may not capture all retracted papers.
The study does not provide a causal analysis of the factors leading to retractions.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure the robustness of the findings, the study used descriptive statistics and visualizations to summarize the data.

arXiv_2604.24071v1.title: PeeriScope: A Multi-Faceted Framework for Evaluating Peer Review Quality
arXiv_2604.24071v1.venue: Companion Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW Companion '26)
arXiv_2604.24071v1.research_idea: PeeriScope is a modular platform that integrates structured features, rubric-guided large language model assessments, and supervised prediction to evaluate peer review quality along multiple dimensions. It is designed for openness and integration, providing a public interface and a documented API.
arXiv_2604.24071v1.research_question: How can peer review quality be systematically, interpretable, and extensibly assessed using a multi-faceted framework?
hypotheses: PeeriScope can effectively evaluate peer review quality along multiple dimensions using structured linguistic metrics, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling.
PeeriScope can provide practical deployment and research extensibility through its public interface and documented API.
arXiv_2604.24071v1.data_description: OpenAlex is used to retrieve reviewer publication profiles and derive topical-expertise and citation features. The data includes reviewer publication history, citation counts, and scholarly tenure.

independent_variables.name: Review Length
independent_variables.definition: The length of the review text, reported as a proxy for thoroughness.

independent_variables.name: Hedging
independent_variables.definition: Measured using a cue-based neural detector, captures the epistemic stance of the reviewer.

independent_variables.name: Lexical Diversity
independent_variables.definition: Measured as type-token ratio, reflects linguistic variation and effort.

independent_variables.name: Readability
independent_variables.definition: Captured using the Flesch Reading Ease score, reflects how easily the review can be understood.

independent_variables.name: Politeness
independent_variables.definition: Linked to perceptions of fairness and author receptivity.

independent_variables.name: Sentiment
independent_variables.definition: Polarity offers a coarse but informative indicator of evaluative direction.

independent_variables.name: Similarity
independent_variables.definition: General similarity of the review to the manuscript reflects the reviewer’s overall domain proximity.

independent_variables.name: Citations
independent_variables.definition: Mentions of citations provide support for reviewers’ claims and help ground them.

independent_variables.name: Questions
independent_variables.definition: Presence of questions in the review, indicating constructive feedback.

independent_variables.name: Topical Alignment
independent_variables.definition: Measured using SPECTER, reflects the alignment between the submission and the reviewer’s publication history.

independent_variables.name: Citation Count
independent_variables.definition: Characterizes the reviewer’s standing through citation count.

independent_variables.name: Scholarly Tenure
independent_variables.definition: Proxy for influence within a field, derived from the reviewer’s publication history.

dependent_variables.name: Review Quality Score
dependent_variables.formula: Integrated score derived from structured metrics, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling.

statistical_method.specification: PeeriScope uses a combination of structured linguistic metrics, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling to evaluate peer review quality.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable

grouping_strategy.rationale: Not applicable

conclusion_claims: PeeriScope provides a multi-faceted framework for evaluating peer review quality using structured features, LLM-based scoring, and supervised modeling.
PeeriScope is designed for openness and integration, providing a public interface and a documented API for practical deployment and research extensibility.
limitations: Peer-review datasets are inherently sparse, fragmented across venues, and largely inaccessible due to confidentiality constraints.
Evaluating LLM-assisted practices remains difficult due to the lack of annotated, high-quality review data.

_source.title: PeeriScope: A Multi-Faceted Framework for Evaluating Peer Review Quality

suebsombut2023_smart_farm.title: The using of bibliometric analysis to classify trends and future directions on 'Smart Farm'
suebsombut2023_smart_farm.research_idea: This study explores the research trends and identifies research clusters on smart farm using bibliometric analysis to support farming and improve the quality of farm production.
suebsombut2023_smart_farm.research_question: What are the research trends and clusters in the field of smart farm, and how can they inform future research directions?
suebsombut2023_smart_farm.data_source: Web of Science (WOS) and Scopus online database
suebsombut2023_smart_farm.data_description: The study examines selected research articles in the smart farm field, focusing on keywords related to smart farm, agriculture, supply chain, knowledge management, traceability, and product lifecycle management.

statistical_method.specification: Bibliometric analysis is used to explore the relationship of the articles from a co-citation network of the articles, and science mapping is used to identify clusters in the relationship.

network_construction.construction_method: The co-citation network of the articles is used to explore the relationship of the articles, and science mapping is used to identify clusters in the relationship.

groups: soil carbon emission from farming activity
grouping_strategy.rationale: The area of research in smart farm is categorized into two clusters based on the VOSviewer tool with keywords related to research articles.

result_claims.metric: major cluster of smart farm research
result_claims.value: soil carbon emission from farming activity

partial_results.note: The study identifies two clusters but does not provide detailed statistical significance for the clusters.

conclusion_claims: The major cluster of smart farm research is the soil carbon emission from farming activity, which impacts on climate change that affects food production and productivity.
The contribution is to identify the trends on smart farm to develop research in the future by means of bibliometric analysis.
limitations: The study does not provide detailed statistical significance for the identified research clusters.
The time period of the data is not specified.

Scientometrics_2603.19319v1.title: Exploring Novelty Differences between Industry and Academia: A Knowledge Entity-centric Perspective
Scientometrics_2603.19319v1.research_idea: This study investigates the differences in novelty between academic and industrial research outputs by leveraging a unified semantic space to analyze fine-grained knowledge entities (Methods, Tools, Datasets, Metrics) in both patents and papers. It provides a generalizable framework for novelty comparison across different types of literature.
Scientometrics_2603.19319v1.research_question: Is there a difference in the novelty of scientific and technical literature between industry and academia?
hypotheses: Academic research outputs will demonstrate higher novelty compared to industry outputs.

The relationship between funding and scientific output/impact will exhibit diminishing marginal returns.
mongeon2023_concentration.data_source: Funding data from research councils in Québec, combined with publication and citation data.
mongeon2023_concentration.data_description: The study analyzes data on 12,720 researchers in Québec over a 15-year period (1998-2012), with scientific output and impact measured from 2000 to 2013.

filters: Researchers in Québec who received funding from national and provincial research councils

independent_variables.name: funding_amount
independent_variables.definition: The total amount of research funding received by each researcher over the 15-year period.

dependent_variables.name: scientific_output
dependent_variables.formula: Number of scientific papers produced by each researcher from 2000 to 2013.

dependent_variables.name: scientific_impact
dependent_variables.formula: Citation impact of each researcher's publications from 2000 to 2013.

control_variables.name: research_field
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in output and impact across scientific fields.

control_variables.name: career_stage
control_variables.rationale: To account for differences in productivity and impact based on the researcher's career stage.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and regression analysis to examine the relationship between funding and scientific output and impact.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the scientific output and impact of researchers who received the most funding versus those who received less funding.

result_claims.value: do not stand out in terms of output and impact

partial_results.note: The study does not provide detailed results on how the relationship between funding and output/impact varies across specific fields.

conclusion_claims: The concentration of research funding in the hands of a few researchers generally produces diminishing marginal returns in terms of scientific output and impact.
The most funded researchers do not necessarily produce the highest scientific output or impact.
limitations: The study is limited to researchers in Québec and may not be generalizable to other regions or countries.
The study does not account for all potential confounding factors that may influence scientific output and impact.

robustness_checks.rationale: To verify whether the results hold across different scientific fields and career stages.

hypotheses: There is a significant correlation between journal quartiles and the number of retracted papers.

dependent_variables.name: average_citations_from_retracted_papers

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in retraction rates and citation behavior.

control_variables.name: field_of_study
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in citation practices across disciplines.

statistical_method.specification: Descriptive statistics and comparative analysis of citation patterns between retracted and unretracted papers.

arXiv_2104.05562v1.research_question: Can the collaboration patterns of an author predict their future ℎ-index?

Graph neural networks can outperform traditional machine learning methods in predicting the ℎ-index.

independent_variables.name: textual_content

partial_results.note: Textual features generated from the author's published papers do not provide much information.

conclusion_claims: The structural role of an author in the co-authorship network is empirically related to their future ℎ-index.

limitations: The study is limited to the field of computer science.
The predictive power of textual features is limited.

robustness_checks.method: comparison with standard machine learning techniques
robustness_checks.rationale: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed GNN approach.

Training an image classifier with LICO consistently improves classification accuracy.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable

robustness_checks.method: Comparison with multiple interpretability methods (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, RISE)
robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the results are not dependent on a single interpretability method.

schueler2023_platform_research.title: Objectives of platform research: A co-citation and systematic literature review analysis

schueler2023_platform_research.data_description: 10,357 research papers related to digital platforms from the Web of Science database. A subset of 107 papers was analyzed in the platform ecosystem research field.

arXiv_2105.06840v1.title: The academic wanderer: structure of collaboration network and relation with research performance
arXiv_2105.06840v1.research_idea: This study explores the relationship between the structure of scientists' co-authorship ego networks, academic performance, and academic mobility, leveraging cognitive models from anthropology to understand how cognitive investment in collaborations affects research outcomes.
arXiv_2105.06840v1.research_question: How do the structure of co-authorship ego networks, academic performance, and academic mobility interrelate across different career stages?
hypotheses: There is a positive correlation between academic performance (productivity and impact) and the size of co-authorship ego network layers.
Academic mobility (both international and domestic) is associated with larger collaboration networks.
A cognitive capacity bound exists in the size of collaboration networks, similar to social networks.
International and domestic migrants differ in their efficiency in exploiting collaboration networks for productivity and impact.

independent_variables.name: academic_mobility

independent_variables.name: ego_network_structure

dependent_variables.name: ego_network_layer_size

dependent_variables.name: research_impact

control_variables.name: field_of_study
control_variables.rationale: To control for variations in collaboration norms and research impact across disciplines.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare how different types of academic mobility affect the structure and effectiveness of collaboration networks.

key_quantities: correlation between ego network layer sizes and academic performance
comparison of ego network growth between international and domestic migrants

result_claims.metric: correlation between ego network layer sizes and academic performance
capacity bound effect on ego network size
international migrants vs. domestic migrants in ego network growth
result_claims.value: International migrants grow larger networks, domestic migrants achieve higher impact

conclusion_claims: There is a clear correlation between the structure of co-authorship ego networks and academic performance indices, with higher productivity and impact associated with larger networks.

arXiv_2605.12514v1.title: Structural Diversity Drives Disruptive Scientific Innovation
arXiv_2605.12514v1.research_idea: The paper introduces Structural Diversity (SD) as a new metric to measure the extent to which a team bridges distinct knowledge communities in its prior collaboration network. It demonstrates that SD is a robust predictor of disruptive innovation in scientific research, outperforming traditional metrics like team size and team freshness.
arXiv_2605.12514v1.research_question: What organizational structure fosters disruptive scientific innovation, and how does Structural Diversity (SD) influence this process?
hypotheses: Structural Diversity (SD) is a robust predictor of disruptive innovation in scientific research.
SD positively interacts with team size, mitigating the 'curse of scale' in large teams.
SD drives disruptive innovation by fostering disciplinary integration.
arXiv_2605.12514v1.data_description: A century-scale dataset of 260 million scientific publications spanning 19 disciplines from 1900 to 2025.

independent_variables.name: Structural Diversity (SD)
independent_variables.definition: The weighted number of connected components (CCs) in a team's historical co-authorship network, representing the extent to which a team bridges distinct knowledge communities.

independent_variables.name: Team Size
independent_variables.definition: The number of authors in a team, log-transformed to account for skewed distribution.

independent_variables.name: Team Freshness
independent_variables.definition: The proportion of previously unconnected collaborators in a team.

independent_variables.name: Edge Density
independent_variables.definition: The density of connections among team members in their prior collaboration network.

dependent_variables.name: CD Index
dependent_variables.formula: A field-normalized index measuring the disruptive impact of a scientific paper.

control_variables.name: Title Word Count
control_variables.rationale: To account for the length of the paper title as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Title Readability Score
control_variables.rationale: To account for the readability of the paper title as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Title Promotional Words (%)
control_variables.rationale: To account for the presence of promotional language in the paper title as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Author Career Age
control_variables.rationale: To account for the career stage of authors as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Author Career Age Squared
control_variables.rationale: To capture the non-linear effect of author career age on innovation behaviors.

control_variables.name: Author Institution h-index
control_variables.rationale: To account for the prestige of the author's institution as a potential confounder.

control_variables.name: Author Number of Publications (log)
control_variables.rationale: To account for the productivity of authors as a potential confounder.

statistical_method.specification: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression models with fixed effects for discipline and publication year.

network_construction.construction_method: Based on the historical co-authorship network of team members prior to a focal publication.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the disruptive innovation outcomes of teams with varying levels of structural diversity.

key_quantities: coefficient of SD on CD Index
interaction effect between SD and team size

result_claims.metric: Interaction between SD and Team Size

partial_results.metric: Effect of SD on CD Index
partial_results.note: The effect of SD on CD Index is robust even after controlling for key confounding factors.

conclusion_claims: Structural Diversity (SD) is a powerful and robust predictor of disruptive innovation in scientific research.
SD positively interacts with team size, transforming team scale from a liability into a resource for creative synthesis.
limitations: The study relies on historical co-authorship data, which may not fully capture the cognitive diversity of team members.

robustness_checks.rationale: To account for potential confounding variables and ensure the causal interpretation of the results.

robustness_checks.method: Quasi-natural experiment based on a U.S. National Science Foundation (NSF) policy change in 2012
robustness_checks.rationale: To provide additional evidence for the causal effect of SD on disruptive innovation.

_source.title: Structural Diversity Drives Disruptive Scientific Innovation

lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.title: From Newborn to Impact: Bias-Aware Citation Prediction
lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.research_idea: The paper proposes a Bias-Aware Citation Prediction Framework (BA-Cite) that combines multi-agent feature extraction with robust graph representation learning to address the challenges of predicting citations for newborn papers, particularly in the presence of long-tailed distributions and implicit factors.
lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.research_question: How can we design bias-aware models that deliver stable predictions on low-citation papers while revealing the underlying drivers of scientific impact?
hypotheses: Prior citation predictors overfit explicit signals, leading to degradation on long-tail papers and under distribution shifts.
A multi-source informed graph learning framework can enable robust prediction even without early citation information.
lu2026_bias_aware_citation_prediction.data_description: Two large-scale academic datasets containing metadata about papers, authors, institutions, venues, and related descriptors. The datasets are used to evaluate the proposed framework.

independent_variables.name: reproducibility
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how reproducible a paper's findings are, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: collaboration_network
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the collaboration structure of the authors, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: text_quality
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the quality of the paper's text, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: topic_hotness
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how hot or trending the paper's topic is, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: venue_prestige
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the prestige of the venue where the paper was published, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: author_reputation
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the reputation of the authors, derived from metadata and external resources.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: The number of citations a paper receives over time.

control_variables.name: publication_year
control_variables.rationale: To account for temporal trends in citation behavior.

control_variables.name: number_of_authors
control_variables.rationale: To account for the potential influence of collaboration size on citation count.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a multi-agent × graph co-learning module to derive fine-grained, interpretable signals and fuses them with heterogeneous-network embeddings. It also incorporates a two-stage forward process, GroupDRO, and a regularization head for robust learning.

network_construction.construction_method: Heterogeneous academic graphs are constructed using metadata and external resources, with edges representing citations between papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To evaluate the performance of the model on different citation groups and ensure robustness on lowly cited papers.

partial_results.metric: Model performance on lowly cited papers
partial_results.note: The paper does not provide detailed results on the performance of the model on lowly cited papers beyond the overall metrics.

conclusion_claims: The proposed Bias-Aware Citation Prediction Framework (BA-Cite) achieves significant improvements in citation prediction for newborn papers.
The framework addresses the research gaps of insufficient modeling of implicit factors and a lack of bias-aware learning, leading to more robust and accurate predictions.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed information on the specific datasets used beyond their names.

asatani2025_influential_scientists.title: Influential scientists shape knowledge flows between science and IGO policy
asatani2025_influential_scientists.research_idea: The paper investigates how scientific research influences intergovernmental organization (IGO) policy by identifying a small group of policy-influential scientists (PI-Sci) who dominate the knowledge flow from science to policy. It reveals how network structure and elite brokerage shape the translation of research into global policy.
asatani2025_influential_scientists.research_question: What are the mechanisms through which scientific research is translated into IGO policy, and who are the key individuals and networks that dominate this process?
hypotheses: A small group of scientists (PI-Sci) disproportionately influences IGO policy through their co-authorship networks and prior policy citations.
The concentration of influence varies by scientific field, with more established fields showing tighter clustering of influence.
Policy citations are influenced by factors such as journal selection, citation of policy-relevant research, and authors' prior policy citations.
asatani2025_influential_scientists.data_source: Overton, Scopus, and IGO policy documents
asatani2025_influential_scientists.data_description: The study analyzes 230,737 scientific papers cited in IGO policy documents from 2015 to 2023. These papers are linked to their authors and co-authorship networks using Scopus author identifiers and Overton records.

filters: Papers cited in IGO policy documents

independent_variables.name: journal_of_publication
independent_variables.definition: The journal in which the paper was published, used as a proxy for academic impact and policy relevance.

independent_variables.name: policy_cited_references
independent_variables.definition: The number of references in a paper that have been previously cited in IGO policy documents.

independent_variables.name: authors_past_policy_citations
independent_variables.definition: The number of policy-cited papers previously authored by any co-author of the paper.

independent_variables.name: country_of_origin
independent_variables.definition: The country of the author's affiliation, used to analyze regional disparities in policy citation rates.

dependent_variables.name: policy_citation
dependent_variables.formula: 1 if the paper is cited in an IGO policy document, 0 otherwise.

dependent_variables.name: citation_distance
dependent_variables.formula: The shortest citation-path distance from the paper to an IGO policy document.

control_variables.name: academic_citation_count
control_variables.rationale: To account for the general academic impact of a paper, which may influence its likelihood of being cited in policy.

control_variables.name: number_of_authors
control_variables.rationale: To control for the potential influence of collaboration size on policy citation likelihood.

statistical_method.specification: The study uses descriptive statistics and network analysis to examine the distribution of policy citations and the structure of co-authorship networks among policy-influential scientists.

network_construction.construction_method: Co-authorship networks are constructed based on the authors of policy-cited papers, with edges representing collaborative relationships.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the characteristics and influence of scientists who receive a disproportionate share of policy citations with those who do not.

key_quantities: Policy citation probability by journal volume
Policy citation probability by number of policy-cited references

result_claims.value: 93.3% of papers are connected to IGO policy documents within 3 or 4 steps in the citation network.
Papers citing more than eight policy-cited references have a 30% chance of subsequent policy citation.
A small group of scientists (PI-Sci) receive a disproportionate share of policy citations and collaborate within dense international networks.

partial_results.metric: Concentration of influence in emerging fields
partial_results.note: The concentration of influence is weaker in rapidly evolving areas like data science and AI compared to established fields like climate modeling.

conclusion_claims: A small group of policy-influential scientists (PI-Sci) dominate the flow of scientific knowledge into IGO policy, particularly in established fields.
The structure of co-authorship networks and the prior policy influence of authors are key factors in determining the likelihood of policy citation.
limitations: The study focuses on IGO policy documents and may not capture the full scope of science-policy interactions in other contexts.
The analysis is based on citation data, which may not fully reflect the actual impact of scientific research on policy decisions.

robustness_checks.rationale: To ensure that the observed patterns in co-authorship networks are not artifacts of the network construction method.
To confirm that the identified factors (journal selection, policy-cited references, and authors' prior policy citations) consistently predict policy citation likelihood.

wu2026_persistent_geographical_biases.title: Persistent geographical biases in global scientific collaboration and citations

National borders and country-specific biases influence scientific collaboration and citation patterns.
International mobility increases collaboration but has limited effects on citation flows.

abramo2013_assessing_accuracy.research_idea: The paper evaluates the accuracy of the h-index and g-index in measuring individual researchers' productivity by comparing them to a more accurate indicator, the fractional scientific strength (FSS).

The approach allows for capturing the impact of technological change on prior technologies, which is not detectable with traditional citation-based measures.
funk2017_dynamic_network.research_question: How do new inventions reshape the network of interlinked technologies, and what factors influence whether they consolidate or destabilize the status quo?
hypotheses: Federal research funding pushes universities to create inventions that are more destabilizing.

Higher for universities with commercial engagement

thelwall2025_openalex_citation.title: Is OpenAlex Suitable for Research Quality Evaluation and Which Citation Indicator is Best?
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.research_idea: This paper evaluates the suitability of OpenAlex for research quality evaluation and compares the effectiveness of different citation-based indicators (raw counts, NCS, NLCS) against expert quality assessments.
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.research_question: Which citation-based indicator and database (OpenAlex or Scopus) best correlates with research quality as judged by expert evaluations?
hypotheses: OpenAlex citation counts are better for research quality indicators than Scopus citation counts.
Field-normalized indicators (NCS or NLCS) are better than raw citation counts for evaluating research quality.
The broadest OpenAlex classification scheme provides the best indicators.
OpenAlex's inclusive document classification compromises the value of field normalization.
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.data_source: OpenAlex, Scopus, and UK Research Excellence Framework (REF) 2021 data
thelwall2025_openalex_citation.data_description: 28.6 million articles from 2014-2020, with 97,816 UK REF2021 articles used as gold standards. Two gold standards: ChatGPT scores and average REF2021 expert review scores.

filters: articles in both OpenAlex and Scopus

independent_variables.name: citation_count
independent_variables.definition: Number of citations received by an article

independent_variables.name: NCS
independent_variables.definition: Normalised Citation Score, calculated as citation count divided by the mean citation count of all articles in the same field and year

independent_variables.name: NLCS
independent_variables.definition: Normalised Log-transformed Citation Score, calculated as ln(1 + citation count) divided by the mean ln(1 + citation count) of all articles in the same field and year

independent_variables.name: database

independent_variables.name: classification_scheme
independent_variables.definition: Subject classification scheme used (Scopus broad fields, OpenAlex domains, fields, subfields, or topics)

dependent_variables.formula: Quality score assigned by ChatGPT for each article

control_variables.name: field

control_variables.name: document_type

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable

arXiv_2510.19246v2.title: From Newborn to Impact: Bias-Aware Citation Prediction
arXiv_2510.19246v2.research_idea: The paper proposes a Bias-Aware Citation Prediction Framework (BA-Cite) that combines multi-agent feature extraction with robust graph representation learning to address the challenges of predicting citations for newborn papers, particularly in the presence of long-tailed distributions and implicit factors.
arXiv_2510.19246v2.research_question: How can we design bias-aware models that deliver stable predictions on low-citation papers while revealing the underlying drivers of scientific impact?
hypotheses: Prior citation predictors overfit explicit signals, leading to degradation on long-tail papers and under distribution shifts.
A multi-source informed graph learning framework can enable robust prediction even without early citation information.
arXiv_2510.19246v2.data_description: Two large-scale academic datasets containing metadata about papers, authors, institutions, venues, and citation networks.

independent_variables.name: reproducibility
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how reproducible a paper's findings are, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: collaboration_network
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the collaboration structure of the authors, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: text_quality
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the quality of the paper's text, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: topic_hotness
independent_variables.definition: A measure of how hot or trending the paper's topic is, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: venue_prestige
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the prestige of the venue where the paper was published, derived from metadata and external resources.

independent_variables.name: author_reputation
independent_variables.definition: A measure of the reputation of the authors, derived from metadata and external resources.

dependent_variables.name: citation_count
dependent_variables.formula: The number of citations a paper receives over a given time period.

control_variables.name: early_citation_signals
control_variables.rationale: To account for the influence of early citations on the prediction model.

statistical_method.specification: The paper uses a combination of multi-agent feature extraction and robust graph representation learning to model citation prediction.

network_construction.construction_method: Heterogeneous-network embeddings are used to represent the citation network, incorporating metadata and external resources.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To evaluate the performance of the model on different citation groups and ensure robustness on lowly cited papers.

conclusion_claims: The proposed Bias-Aware Citation Prediction Framework (BA-Cite) achieves significant improvements in citation prediction for newborn papers.
The model reduces error metrics by around 13% and improves the ranking metric by 5.5% compared to baseline methods.
limitations: The paper does not provide detailed information about the time window of the data.
The paper does not discuss the generalizability of the model to other domains or types of academic content.

robustness_checks.rationale: To optimize worst-case group risk across environments and ensure robustness on lowly cited papers.
To perform what-if analyses on controllable factors under monotonicity and smoothness constraints.

_source.title: From Newborn to Impact: Bias-Aware Citation Prediction

Research_Policy_2602.18940v1.title: DREAM: Deep Research Evaluation with Agentic Metrics
Research_Policy_2602.18940v1.research_idea: The paper introduces DREAM, a framework for evaluating deep research agents (DRAs) that addresses the 'Mirage of Synthesis' by incorporating agentic metrics and active verification. DREAM combines static and adaptive metrics to assess research quality across four verticals: Presentation Quality, Task Compliance, Analytical Depth, and Source Quality.
Research_Policy_2602.18940v1.research_question: How can deep research agents be evaluated effectively, considering the multidimensional nature of research quality and the limitations of existing benchmarks?
hypotheses: Current DRE benchmarks suffer from the 'Mirage of Synthesis' due to their inability to detect factual, temporal, and logical degradation.
DREAM's agentic evaluation framework will be more sensitive to factual and temporal decay than existing benchmarks.

zheng2025_social_media_retraction.title: Can social media provide early warning of retraction? Evidence from critical tweets identified by human annotation and large language models
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.venue: Journal of the Association for Information Science and Technology
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.research_idea: This study explores whether social media commentary, particularly critical tweets, can serve as an early warning signal for retracted scholarly articles. It combines human annotation and large language models (LLMs) to identify critical tweets and assess their potential as indicators of problematic research.
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.research_question: Can social media commentary, specifically critical tweets, serve as an early warning signal for scholarly articles that are later retracted?
hypotheses: Retracted articles are more likely to be associated with critical tweets than non-retracted articles.
Human annotation and LLMs will identify similar patterns of critical tweets related to retracted articles.
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.data_source: Twitter, Crossref, and journal retraction databases
zheng2025_social_media_retraction.data_description: The study analyzed 3,815 tweets referencing 604 retracted articles and 3,373 tweets referencing 668 non-retracted articles. The tweets were collected from Twitter and annotated for critical content.

sample_scope.time_window: Not explicitly specified, but data collection occurred before 2025
filters: Tweets referencing retracted and non-retracted articles

independent_variables.name: retraction_status
independent_variables.definition: Whether the article was retracted (1) or not (0)

independent_variables.name: critical_tweet
independent_variables.definition: Whether the tweet was identified as critical of the article (1) or not (0)

dependent_variables.name: critical_tweet_association
dependent_variables.formula: Whether an article was associated with at least one critical tweet prior to retraction (1) or not (0)

control_variables.name: article_publication_date
control_variables.rationale: To account for the time between article publication and tweet posting

control_variables.name: article_field
control_variables.rationale: To control for differences in disciplinary context that may influence social media engagement

statistical_method.specification: The study primarily uses descriptive statistics to compare the proportion of retracted and non-retracted articles associated with critical tweets.

network_construction.construction_method: Not applicable

grouping_strategy.rationale: To compare the likelihood of critical tweets being associated with retracted versus non-retracted articles.

key_quantities: Proportion of retracted articles with critical tweets
Proportion of non-retracted articles with critical tweets

result_claims.metric: Proportion of retracted articles with critical tweets
Proportion of non-retracted articles with critical tweets
Alignment between human annotation and LLMs

partial_results.metric: LLM performance in identifying critical tweets
partial_results.note: LLMs only partially aligned with human annotation, suggesting limitations in fully automated monitoring.

conclusion_claims: Critical tweets identified by human annotation are more common for retracted articles than for non-retracted articles, suggesting their potential as early warning signals.
A human-AI collaborative approach may offer a more reliable and scalable method for monitoring post-publication discourse and identifying problematic research.
limitations: The study relies on a relatively small sample of retracted and non-retracted articles.
The use of LLMs for identifying critical tweets may introduce noise or miss context, highlighting the need for human oversight.

bak_coleman2026_industry_influence.research_question: To what extent is social media research independent from industry influence?
hypotheses: A significant proportion of high-profile social media research has ties to industry.
These ties are often undisclosed in published research.
Industry-tied research garners more attention and influence than independent research.
Industry ties are associated with a shift in research focus away from platform-scale features.

arXiv_2012.05742v2.title: Longitudinal Citation Prediction using Temporal Graph Neural Networks
arXiv_2012.05742v2.research_idea: The paper introduces the task of sequence citation prediction, where the goal is to predict the trajectory of citation counts over time for scholarly works. It proposes a model that combines graph convolutional networks and LSTMs to exploit both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks.
arXiv_2012.05742v2.research_question: Can leveraging both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks improve the accuracy of predicting citation counts over time?
hypotheses: Modeling a paper's citation network is useful for predicting citation counts over time.
Leveraging both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks will significantly improve the performance of predicting citation counts over time.
arXiv_2012.05742v2.data_description: The dataset contains a dynamic citation network spanning over 42 years, with an updated graph for each year. It includes information about papers, their citations, authors, venues, and abstracts.

statistical_method.specification: An encoder-decoder model combining graph convolutional networks (GCNs) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks to model both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks.

network_construction.construction_method: A dynamic citation network is constructed from the Semantic Scholar dataset, with an updated graph for each year. Each node represents a paper, and edges represent citations between papers.

grouping_strategy.rationale: To test the importance of topological and temporal information in predicting citation counts over time.

partial_results.note: The exact performance metrics are not provided in the text.

conclusion_claims: Leveraging both topological and temporal information in dynamic citation networks greatly increases the performance of predicting citation counts over time.
The proposed encoder-decoder model combining GCNs and LSTMs is effective for sequence citation prediction.
limitations: The paper does not provide specific performance metrics for the proposed model.
The paper does not discuss potential limitations of the model or the data.

robustness_checks.rationale: To test the importance of topological and temporal information in the model.
